Author: Kanhaiya Jagnani
Title: Lessons From 13 Years in Performance Engineering
Text:
Lessons From 13 Years in Performance Engineering
— the beginning of a journey
Hi,
I have been working as a performance engineer for the last 13 years, across different systems and teams, and over time I’ve realized something important.
Nowadays, performance engineering (or performance testing) is mostly understood as:
• executing a set of tests with some load
• collecting performance numbers
• finding a cost-effective (often free) tool to write scripts
• automating those scripts
• and finally configuring everything into a pipeline that runs at regular intervals
For many people who are not deeply connected with performance engineering, this is what they expect the job to be.
But according to me, this is where the real problem lies.
Speed Is Increasing, Understanding Is Reducing
With AI and automation becoming mainstream, the expectation from any technical professional is to deliver results very quickly.
While speed is important, this mindset often weakens the core of the performance engineering process.
Let me explain this with a simple example.
A Tale of Two Performance Engineers
Consider two performance engineers — Alex and Sam — working on a product release where a couple of new features have been introduced. Both are asked to validate the performance of these features.
Alex’s approach
Alex starts by understanding the feature at a surface level.
Using historical data, he creates a workload model, scripts the test scenarios, executes the tests, and prepares a performance report.
As a proactive contributor, he then automates the entire flow, creates a Jenkins pipeline, and schedules it to run at regular intervals.
On paper, this looks like a perfect delivery.
Sam’s approach
Sam begins differently.
He first reaches out to the architects to understand why the new feature was introduced:
• How frequently will it be used in production?
• What is its business and technical criticality?
• What kind of backend interactions does it trigger?
Based on this understanding, he creates a workload model and executes the performance tests.
But he doesn’t stop there.
Along with test execution, he:
• analyzes network sessions
• captures thread dumps and heap dumps
• studies backend processing behavior
During this deeper analysis, Sam identifies an illogical backend implementation — something that does not immediately appear in response time or throughput metrics, but could later become a serious issue in real production scenarios.
Sam spends significant time uncovering this insight.
As a result, he does not get enough time to automate the flow or build a pipeline.
A Question Worth Asking
Now ask yourself:
Who do you think will be appreciated by management?
And more importantly — who actually did the performance engineering job properly?
Definitions vs Understanding
When I ask newly joined performance engineers questions like:
• Why do we use the 90th percentile?
• What is the real significance of think time or pacing?
Most of the time, I get textbook definitions.
I smile — and ask again.
Because I am not asking for definitions.
I am asking for understanding.
This gap exists because we often push teams to deliver faster, but rarely emphasize:
• why a process exists
• what problem it is solving
• what risks appear when it is ignored
As a result, people start following processes mechanically, without understanding their real significance.
Why I’m Writing This
This post marks the beginning of a journey.
Through these writings, I want to share my experiences, learnings, and observations from the world of performance engineering — accumulated over years of real systems, real constraints, and real trade-offs.
This is not about tools.
This is not about scripts.
This is about thinking, reasoning, and engineering judgment.
I hope to find a few fellow travelers along the way —
people who want to truly understand performance engineering, not just execute it.
